summary: Jeff Atwood pushes back against the conventional wisdom that software projects require dedicated, pristine build machines with elaborate documentation and change control. He argues that while controlled build processes have merit, the 'magical build machine' concept creates harmful disconnects between developers and deployment realities. The post contends that if your build process requires special voodoo rituals to work, your project architecture is fundamentally flawed. Atwood emphasizes that every developer should understand how to produce reliable builds from their own machines and troubleshoot deployment issues. He notes that modern .NET deployment should be simple enough that elaborate build machine rituals are unnecessary.
key_points: Dedicated build machines can isolate developers from understanding deployment, creating an 'us vs them' mentality between development and production
If a project requires magical build machine rituals to compile, it signals architectural problems and 'voodoo programming'
Every developer should be capable of producing and troubleshooting a reliable build from their own workstation
Modern frameworks like .NET were designed for simple xcopy deployment, making elaborate build ceremonies unnecessary
Controlled build processes are valid, but teams should avoid injecting accidental 'magic' into development workflows
money_quotes: It runs on my box! Every developer on the team should understand how to produce a reliable build from their own machine.
If you use a magical build machine, you're implying that your project is so complicated to build that it takes special voodoo to get it to run.
Sacrifice a chicken, sprinkle salt over your shoulder, then re-image the build machine when the stars are perfectly aligned. That's the only way to get a 'clean' build!
A project that is this difficult to build does not inspire confidence. It also smacks of voodoo programming or programming by coincidence.
It is every developer's responsibility to write responsible code, code that doesn't cause a lot of deployment problems.
Use a build machine if it makes sense for your project, but be careful that you aren't injecting any accidental 'magic' into your development process along the way.
key_insight: Build complexity that requires 'magical' dedicated machines often masks architectural problems; every developer should be able to build and deploy from their own workstation.
